coredata.dc:title: The effect of steric hindrance on FRET efficiency in carbon dots for fluorescence regulation and stability enhancement
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Xiao, Jun
Jiang, Yuan
Lu, Yutong
Zheng, Hui
Zheng, Peng
Zheng, Liang
Zhang, Yang
coredata.dc:description: Surface modification can influence the optical properties of nanomaterials. In this study, we synthesized tunable dual-emissive carbon dots (CDs) by changing the amount of methyl in the starting materials. With higher methyl content, the spatial resistance increase, which in turn diminished Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) efficiency between the core states and surface states of the CDs. This results in a decrease in green fluorescence intensity and a relative dominance of blue fluorescence. The FRET effect was systematically investigated through characterizations and calculations. Beneficial from high steric hindrance, CDs derived from 2-amino-2-methyl-1-propanol (CDs-AMP) with multi-branches exhibited high stability under various conditions, including prolonged storage, UV exposure, extreme pH levels, and high-concentration salt solutions. Based on this, a probe for detecting Fe3+ ions was constructed and applied in real samples including mineral, tap, and lake waters. The fluorescence intensity was linear with the concentration of Fe3+ ions in the range of 0–40 μM, with the detection limit of 0.2 μM.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
FRET
Surface modification
Steric hindrance
Ions detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of methyl groups on FRET process

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (a) CDs-EA, (b) CDs-MIPA, (c) CDs-BMAE, and (d) CDs-AMP, respectively.
FTIR spectra of CDs-AMP, CDs-BMAE, CDs-MIPA, CDs-EA.
XPS (i) full spectral scans, and high-resolution spectra of (ii) C1s, (iii) N1s, and (iv) O1s spectra of (A) CDs-EA, (B) CDs-MIPA, (C) CDs-BMAE, and (D) CDs-AMP.
PL spectra of (a) CDs-EA, (b) CDs-MIPA, (c) CDs-BMAE, and (d) CDs-AMP, respectively.
Absorbance and PLE spectra of (a) CDs-EA, (b) CDs-MIPA, (c) CDs-BMAE, (d) CDs-AMP.
(a) Schematic overview of the microstructures of the four CDs. (b) Schematic mechanism of the FRET efficiency of the four CDs.
Fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs-AMP under (a) storage time, (b) UV radiation, (c) pH level, (d) salt solution.

(a) Effect of possible interfering ions on Fe3+ detection. (b)Fluorescence emission spectra of CD-AMP solutions following the addition of various concentrations of Fe3+. Inset: The linear relationship between (F0 − F)/F0 and Fe3+.
Schematic representation of the synthesis of N-CDs.

head.ce:title: The effect of steric hindrance on FRET efficiency in carbon dots for fluorescence regulation and stability enhancement

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Lab for Nanoelectronics and NanoDevices, Department of Electronics Information, Hangzhou Dianzi University, Hangzhou 310018, Zhejiang, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Lab for Nanoelectronics and NanoDevices, Department of Electronics Information, Hangzhou Dianzi University, Hangzhou 310018, Zhejiang, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Micro-nano Sensing and IoT of Wenzhou, Wenzhou Institute of Hangzhou Dianzi University, Wenzhou 325038, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Micro-nano Sensing and IoT of Wenzhou
Wenzhou Institute of Hangzhou Dianzi University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Micro-nano Sensing and IoT of Wenzhou, Wenzhou Institute of Hangzhou Dianzi University, Wenzhou 325038, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Lab for Nanoelectronics and NanoDevices, Department of Electronics Information, Hangzhou Dianzi University, Hangzhou 310018, Zhejiang, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: By adjusting the methyl content of the precursors, four types of carbon dots with different degree of steric hindrances were synthesized, revealing that variations in steric hindrance affect the FRET efficiency between carbon core state and surface state, ultimately leading to the synthesis of carbon dots with high steric hindrance and enhanced stability.

Surface modification can influence the optical properties of nanomaterials. In this study, we synthesized tunable dual-emissive carbon dots (CDs) by changing the amount of methyl in the starting materials. With higher methyl content, the spatial resistance increase, which in turn diminished Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) efficiency between the core states and surface states of the CDs. This results in a decrease in green fluorescence intensity and a relative dominance of blue fluorescence. The FRET effect was systematically investigated through characterizations and calculations. Beneficial from high steric hindrance, CDs derived from 2-amino-2-methyl-1-propanol (CDs-AMP) with multi-branches exhibited high stability under various conditions, including prolonged storage, UV exposure, extreme pH levels, and high-concentration salt solutions. Based on this, a probe for detecting Fe3+ ions was constructed and applied in real samples including mineral, tap, and lake waters. The fluorescence intensity was linear with the concentration of Fe3+ ions in the range of 0–40 μM, with the detection limit of 0.2 μM.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have garnered significant interest due to their ease of preparation, cost-effectiveness, low toxicity, absence of heavy metals, excellent optical properties, and biocompatibility [1–6]. It makes CDs highly attractive for the applications of sensors [7,8], environmental monitoring systems [9,10], and optoelectronic devices [11,12].

CDs exhibit excellent fluorescence properties, such as wide emission ranges, tunable emission wavelengths, high fluorescence quantum yield (QY) and multi-emissions. Currently, the most widely accepted luminescence mechanisms of CDs involve carbon core states, surface/edge states, molecular states, and crosslink-enhanced emission effects [13]. In the case of multi-emission CDs, photoluminescence (PL) mechanisms are quite complex due to the extra coordinated effects of multiple luminescence centers, including intramolecular charge transfer (ICT), excited state intramolecular proton transfer (ESIPT), Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), and so on [14]. Xu et al. prepared CDs with blue and red fluorescence emission. After the post-treatment with ethylenediamine, the increased CN content enhanced the ICT efficiency between the dual emissions, resulting in nearly a fivefold increase in QY and a redshift in the fluorescence peak [15]. Algarra et al. used olive pits as a source for CDs nanomaterials. After acid treatment, CDs were rich in oxygenated functional groups, which facilitated excited-state proton transfer, causing a significant Stokes shift of 144 nm attributed to the ESIPT effect [16]. Bai et al. demonstrated a shift from blue to green emission of CDs by decreasing pH or increasing solvent polarity. The ESIPT phenomenon was structurally supported by intramolecular hydrogen bonding between the closely positioned proton acceptor (CO) and donor (OH) groups [17]. Tan's group investigated the use of Cu (II) as a dopant, which induced a surface state in CDs, offering dual-emission properties. The chelation of the aromatic ring ligand with copper elements promoted the transfer of excited electrons from surface defects to the excited state of Cu2+, producing a new emission [18].

FRET is a non-radiative energy transition process from a donor to an acceptor through intermolecular electric dipole interactions [19,20]. Wu et al. reported dual emission CDs exhibiting a remarkable solvatochromism effect. As solvent polarity decreased, the CDs gradually aggregated leading to increased FRET efficiency and a significant redshift in fluorescence [21]. Wang et al. synthesized color-tunable CDs by altering the filling ratio of the autoclave. The enhancement in FRET efficiency between the dual emission peaks (green/red) leaded to multicolor fluorescence [22]. Feng’s group synthesized the Mn (II)-doped carbon dots, in which intraparticle Förster resonance energy transfer (IPFRET) occurred from the fluorophore-like donor component to the metal-related acceptor component. Owing to the sensitivity of the IPFRET signal, the CDs demonstrated significant potential in sensing applications [23]. Although great efforts have been made in the fluorescence mechanisms and optical control of multi-emissive CDs, the complex nature of the multiple fluorescent centers and their interactions still require further in-depth study.

In this study, we synthesized a series of CDs using a solvothermal method with citric acid and various nitrogen sources in N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF). The nitrogen sources included ethanolamine (EA), amino-2-propanol (MIPA), 1-amino-2-methyl-2-propanol (BMAE), and 2-amino-2-methyl-1-propanol (AMP), resulting in CDs named CDs-EA, CDs-MIPA, CDs-BMAE, and CDs-AMP, respectively (Scheme 1 ). These nitrogen sources possess a two-carbon chain with an amino group (NH2) and a hydroxyl group (OH) at each end, differing in the number and position of methyl groups (CH3). The methyl groups acting as branches on the CD surfaces, result in different spatial hindrance among the CDs. The morphology, microstructure, and composition of the CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). Additionally, the FRET efficiencies of the CDs were calculated. Our results confirmed that FRET interactions from the carbon core state to the surface states are influenced by the steric hindrance. What’s more, the multi-branches of CDs-AMP facilitate higher QY and high stability to storage, pH, UV exposure and salt solution. What’s more, the probe based on CDs-AMP for Fe3+ ions exhibited good selectivity and sensitivity, and had been successfully applied to the detection in real samples.

Citric acid, N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent (Shanghai, China). Ethanolamine (EA), amino-2-propanol (MIPA), 1-Amino-2-methyl-2-propanol (BMAE), 2-Amino-2-methylpropan-1-ol (AMP) were received from Aladdin Reagents Co. (Shanghai, China). All the reagents used were at least of analytical reagent grade unless otherwise specified. Deionized water of 18 MΩ cm−1 was used throughout.

All characterizations were performed at room temperature, PL of the obtained CDs were recorded on a RF-6000 spectro fluorophotometer (Shimadzu Corporation, Japan). UV–vis absorption spectra were measured using a UV-1900i UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Corporation, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) were obtained by a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS20 (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded on a JEM-2100F high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (JEOL, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed on Thermo Scientific K-Alpha (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Lifetime and Quantum yield (QY) assays were conducted by using FLS980 (Edinburgh Instruments, England).

2.3 mM citric acid and 5.5 mM nitrogen-related material were added to 30 mL of DMF and stirred for 10 min. The mixture was then transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated in an oven at 190 °C for 8 h. The nitrogenous materials used were EA, MIPA, BMAE, and AMP, respectively. After cooling to room temperature, the product was centrifuged at 9000 rpm for 30 min to collect the supernatant. The supernatant was then dialyzed using a 500 Da dialysis bag for 2 days, with the water being changed every 4 h to remove unreacted reagents and fluorophores. The obtained samples were stored at 4 °C for further use.
Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer solution, composed of phosphoric acid, acetic acid, and boric acid, was mixed with 0.2 mol/L NaOH solution to prepare buffer solutions with different pH values.

For the photostability test of storage, CDs-AMP solution exposed in air and room temperature for 21 days, and then the solution was diluted in BR buffer solution at pH 6.8. For the photostability test to UV exposure, CDs-AMP were irradiated to 365 nm UV light at different exposition times in air (0–360 min). After exposure, CDs-AMP solution was diluted in BR buffer solution at pH 6.8. For the photostability test to pH, CDs-AMP solution was diluted in BR buffer solution with varying pH values. For the photostability test to salt, CDs-AMP solution was added to NaCl solutions of varying concentrations (0–5 M). After thorough mixing, the mixtures were allowed to stand for 5 min. All the fluorescence spectra of the CDs-AMP solution were measured under an excitation wavelength of 330 nm.

For ion detection, different solutions (40 µM) of Na+, K+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Cr3+, Cd2+, Ag+, Co2+, Mg2+, Fe2+ and Fe3+ were prepared in water. CDs-AMP were mixed with ions solution thoroughly and the fluorescence spectra were recorded after incubating for 5 min at room temperature. CDs-AMP were added into Fe3+ solution with various concentration. Fluorescence measurements were taken at room temperature under the excitation wavelength of 330 nm. The developed method was employed to quantify Fe3+ levels in samples of mineral water, tap water, and lake water. Lake water samples from Yueya Lake (Hangzhou, China) were first filtered using a 0.45 µm membrane filter and subsequently centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 10 min. The tap and mineral water samples required no pretreatment. Various concentrations of Fe3+ were added to the samples, and the analysis was conducted using the proposed approach.

As shown in Scheme 1, CDs were solvothermally prepared in DMF solvent at 190 °C for 8 h using the carbon source citric acid and the nitrogen sources EA, MIPA, BMAE, and AMP, respectively. EA consists of an amino group and a hydroxyl group connected by a two-carbon chain. MIPA has one methyl group at the hydroxyl end, while BMAE has two methyl groups at the hydroxyl end. AMP, on the other hand, has two additional methyl groups at the amino end. The structural variations in these nitrogen sources influence the properties of the synthesized CDs, particularly their spatial configuration and steric hindrance effects.

In terms of morphology and structure, the four kinds of CDs were characterized by TEM, as shown in Fig. 1 . All four CDs possessed a quasi-spherical structure, demonstrated consistent particle sizes, and exhibited a high degree of dispersion. The particle size distribution of the four CDs is shown in the upper left inset, with average sizes calculated to be 3.72 ± 0.28 nm, 3.29 ± 0.30 nm, 3.15 ± 0.25 nm, and 3.37 ± 0.17 nm for CDs-EA, CDs-MIPA, CDs-BMAE, and CDs-AMP, respectively. It can be observed that the sizes of CDs-EA, CDs-MIPA, and CDs-BMAE progressively decrease. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images reveal that all four CDs exhibit clear lattice fringes. The lattice spacings were measured to be 0.24 nm, 0.22 nm, 0.21 nm, and 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (101), (100), (100), and (100) crystal planes of graphite, respectively [24,25].

The FT-IR spectra (Fig. 2 ) provide more insight into the functional groups and elemental states of the CDs. The broad absorption peaks near 3556 and 3486 cm−1 are attributed to the OH and NH stretching vibrations. Vibrational peaks such as CO/CN (1657 cm−1) and CN (1252 cm−1) are observed, indicating the formation of amide/imide bond in the CDs [26,27]. The broad absorption peaks around 1353–1523 cm−1 are caused by the stretching vibrations of CC. Additionally, the absorption peaks at 2927 and 2854 cm−1 are attributed to the CH symmetric and asymmetric stretching, as well as the symmetric bending vibrations of methyl or methylene groups [28]. The peak at 864 cm−1 indicates the presence of CH out-of-plane stretching vibrations, while the absorption at 650 cm−1 suggests the presence of NH oscillating vibrations [29]. It can be seen that the functional group contents of the four CDs are almost identical, indicating that the structure difference of the four CDs are ignorable.

The elemental composition and chemical bonding properties of the four CDs were compared using XPS characterization (Fig. 3 ). The bonding energies of the CDs at 285.2 eV, 532.9 eV and 400.0 eV are attributed to C1s, O1s, and N1s, respectively [15]. The carbon content of CDs-EA, CDs-MIPA, and CDs-BMAE/CDs-AMP increases sequentially, recorded at 69.2 %, 70.3 %, and 73.9/72.7 %, respectively (Table S1). Conversely, the nitrogen content decreases gradually of 8.9 %, 8.6 %, and 7.2/7.9 %. Similarly, the oxygen content also decreases in the order of 21.9 %, 20.9 %, and 18.8/19.4 %. In the high-resolution spectra, the C1s spectrum reveals three peaks at 284.8 eV, 288.1 eV, and 285.8 eV, corresponding to CC/CC, CO/CN, and CO/CN bonds, respectively [30]. The N1s band primarily consists of pyrrole N (399.6 eV), pyridine N (399.0 eV), and a small amount of graphite N (400.7 eV) [31]. The O1s band is dominated by three peaks at 531.7 eV, 533.4 eV, and 529.9 eV, which are attributed to CO, COH/COC, and CO bonds, respectively [32,33]. The bonding nature of the four CDs is consistent, but the proportions vary slightly. The content of CC/CC bonds in CDs-EA, CDs-MIPA, and CDs-BMAE/CDs-AMP gradually increases to 50.1 %, 53.1 %, and 64.5/54.5 %, respectively (Table S2), due to the increase in methyl content in the materials. Additionally, the pyrrolic N content decreases progressively from 78.0 % to 77.2 % and 67.8/67.5 %.

The fluorescence spectra of the four CDs are presented in Fig. 4 . The emission spectra of CDs-EA, CDs-MIPA, and CDs-BMAE all exhibit dual emission peaks, with a primary PL peak around 400–450 nm and a broader PL band around 550 nm. These three types of CDs demonstrate excitation-dependent luminescence, where the blue emission increase and then decrease while the green emission progressively increases with the increase of the excitation wavelength. In contrast, the CDs-AMP displays only a single emission peak at 414 nm, and its fluorescence spectrum shows no dependence on the excitation wavelength. Under 370 nm excitation, the absolute fluorescence QY of CDs-EA, CDs-MIPA, CDs-BMAE, and CDs-AMP were determined to be 4.7 %, 7.6 %, 5.3 %, and 20.0 %, respectively. This indicates that CDs-AMP exhibit the highest QY among the CDs, and the QY of the dual-emission CDs are much lower than that of the single-emissive CDs.

The UV–vis absorption spectra and photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectra of the CDs are shown in Fig. 5 . CDs-EA exhibit a prominent absorption peak at 340 nm, attributed to the n–π* transitions of CC in the carbon structure (Fig. 5a) [34]. In contrast, the according absorption peaks of CDs-MIPA, CDs-BMAE, and CDs-AMP are blue-shifted to 320 nm. Additionally, the shoulder band of CDs-EA at 390 nm and that of CDs-MIPA and CDs-BMAE at 415 nm may be due to the abundant surface functional groups, which is negligible in CDs-AMP [15,35]. The PLE spectra of different fluorescence centers in the four CDs were also investigated. The PLE spectra exhibit n–π* transition bands centered at 377 nm for CDs-EA and at 344 nm for CDs-MIPA, CDs-BMAE, and CDs-AMP, respectively. Moreover, the PLE bands for the green fluorescence centers are much wider compared to those for the blue fluorescence centers. Specifically, CDs-EA has a broad PLE band for green fluorescence ranging from 415 nm to 475 nm, while the maximum excitation peaks for CDs-MIPA and CDs-BMAE are located at 453 nm.

As shown in Fig. S1, the fluorescence decay curves of the four CDs were tested under 370 nm excitation, and fluorescence lifetimes were calculated using double exponential fitting (Table S3). The blue and green emissions of CDs-EA, CDs-MIPA, and CDs-BMAE exhibit different lifetimes, indicating that the two types of transitions originate from distinct PL centers. The blue emission with short lifetime is attributed to the carbon core state, and the green emission with long lifetime is ascribed to the surface state. It is consistent with the two absorption peaks of the absorption spectra mentioned above, corresponding to transitions related to the carbon structure and surface functional groups. While CDs-AMP with only blue emission exhibit one prominent absorption peak.

The average lifetimes (τ) for the blue and green bands are 6.32 ns and 9.20 ns in CDs-EA, 7.59 ns and 8.06 ns in CDs-MIPA, and 8.39 ns and 9.11 ns in CDs-BMAE, respectively. These results show that the average lifetimes of the green bands in the three CDs are consistently longer than those of the blue bands, suggesting the presence of FRET between the two fluorescence centers [35]. The enhancement in average lifetime can be attributed to the competition between the inherently non-radiative FRET process and radiative transitions [36]. Notably, the lifetimes of the blue bands acting as donors in the CDs, increase progressively with the increase in methyl groups. The increasing methyl groups appear to inhibit the FRET process gradually, as the FRET process reduces the donor's lifetime due to competition with radiative transitions. This is also reflected in the fluorescence phenomenon of the three CDs, where the PL intensity of donor emission increases and that of the acceptor decreases with the increase in methyl groups (Fig. 4). Additionally, the FRET process results in a red shift of the fluorescence peak of the acceptor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of methyl groups on FRET process
ce:section.ce:para: FRET is a distance-dependent interaction between the electronic excited states of donors and acceptors [20]. The following three conditions are necessary to achieve FRET: (a) the fluorescence spectrum of CDs and the absorption spectrum of the quencher must overlap, (b) the fluorescence lifetime of CDs must decrease, and (c) the distance between CDs and the quencher must be in the range of 10–100 Å. These conditions are considered to demonstrate the FRET process between the two fluorescence centers in CDs.

The FRET efficiency is influenced by the overlap integral of the donor emission spectrum and the acceptor absorption spectrum. As illustrated in Fig. S2, there is a notable overlap between the donor emission peak at about 450 nm and the acceptor absorption band at 550 nm, indicating the presence of FRET between these two emissions. Furthermore, an increasing overlap between the absorption and emission spectra from CDs-MIPA to CDs-EA was observed, which enhanced the FRET efficiency.

Another important factor influencing the FRET process is the inter-particle distance. The increase in methyl groups on the nitrogen-containing materials in CDs-EA, CDs-MIPA, and CDs-BMAE resulted in enhanced spatial site resistance on the surface of the CDs, as shown in Fig. 6 a. The addition of methyl groups led to an increased distance between the luminescent centers, which weakened the FRET effect of the CDs. Therefore, the blue donor emission increases, and the green acceptor emission decreases with a blue shift in fluorescence. The variation in methyl content caused differing energy transfer efficiencies among the three CDs, which further influenced their photoluminescence (Fig. 6b). Notably, CDs-AMP exhibit only blue emission because the methyl group in AMP is attached to the amino end rather than the hydroxyl end. This configuration suppresses the luminescence of nitrogen-related functional groups in the CDs, leading to the emission being predominantly from the carbon core state in CDs-AMP. Consequently, the surface-state emission is primarily associated with surface states linked to N-related functional groups rather than those linked to O-related functional groups.

To further demonstrate the impact of inter-particle distance on FRET efficiency, we varied the concentration of the CDs solution. Photographs of CDs-EA, CDs-MIPA, and CDs-BMAE in different concentrations under both daylight and 365 nm UV light are shown in Fig. S3. It is evident that the color of the CDs solutions under natural light progressively deepens with increasing concentration. Upon excitation at 365 nm, the fluorescence color transitions from blue to green, accompanied by notable shifts in the primary emission peaks of the three CDs in PL spectra (Fig. S4a–c). At lower concentrations, blue fluorescence is predominant, while at higher concentrations, long-wavelength fluorescence emission becomes dominant. Specifically, at a 1 % concentration, the three CDs solutions exhibit pure blue fluorescence under 365 nm excitation. As the concentration increases to 5 %, the intensity of the blue fluorescence band is enhanced, and a green band peak emerges. When the concentration reaches 10 %, there is a noticeable decrease in blue fluorescence intensity and an increase in green fluorescence intensity. This behavior indicates that increasing the CDs concentration reduces the distance between particles, thereby enhancing FRET efficiency and significantly intensifying acceptor fluorescence. However, when the concentration exceeds 15 %, fluorescence aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) occurs, leading to a reduction in both blue and green emission intensities. At concentrations above 40 %, blue emission nearly vanishes, leaving only green fluorescence. Notably, while the dual peaks of these three types of CDs follow a similar trend, the relative intensities of the dual peaks vary. The normalized fluorescence emission spectra of the three CDs at a 10 % concentration are shown in Fig. S4d, reveal that the green fluorescence intensity of CDs-EA increases more significantly compared to the other two CDs, indicating higher FRET efficiency.

Therefore, it is reasonable to hypothesize that different FRET efficiencies are responsible for the increase in the ratio of green emission relative to blue emission in CDs-BMAE, CDs-MIPA, and CDs-EA. The FRET efficiency (η) can be calculated as:
(1)
η = 1 -
τ
DA
/
τ D
where
τ
DA
refers to the fluorescence lifetime in the presence of an energy acceptor, and
τ D
refers to the fluorescence lifetime in the absence of an energy acceptor. Herein,
τ
DA
corresponds to the fluorescence lifetime of the blue emission of each CDs, while
τ D
refers to the fluorescence lifetime of CDs-AMP, which only exhibits blue fluorescence. The FRET efficiencies of CDs-EA, CDs-MIPA, and CDs-BMAE were calculated to be 48.54 %, 38.16 %, and 31.67 %, respectively. The FRET efficiencies of the three CDs gradually decrease, which is consistent with the fluorescence phenomenon previously demonstrated. These results confirm the presence of a FRET process from the blue band to the green band, which leads to the progressive enhancement of green emission from CDs-BMAE to CDs-EA.

Due to the rich surface functional groups and high sensitivity to environmental changes, CDs are widely used in various detection applications, such as monitoring pH levels and detecting metal ions. However, the sensitivity can also render CDs unstable in different environment, which poses challenges for their practical applications. This instability can hinder their effectiveness in areas such as wastewater treatment [37], biological therapeutics [38], and oil separation [39], where consistent performance under varying conditions is crucial. CDs-AMP, protected by their rich methyl functional groups and multi-branched structures, exhibit remarkable chemical and optical stability. The stability of CDs-AMP was evaluated under multiple conditions: (1) long-term storage; (2) exposure to UV radiation; (3) immersion in both acidic and alkaline environments; and (4) exposure to high-concentration salt solutions.

Over a storage period of 50 days, the fluorescence of CDs-AMP increased slightly, and the PL intensity increased by approximately 8.3 % after eight days (Fig. 7 a). When exposed to 365 nm UV light, the fluorescence intensity of CDs-AMP decreased by only 10.1 % after six hours, indicating strong resistance to photobleaching (Fig. 7b). Across a broad pH range from 5.0 to 13.5, the fluorescence of CDs-AMP remained stable. Even in extremely acidic conditions with a pH of 3.0, the fluorescence intensity retained about 92 % of its original value (Fig. 7c). This remarkable stability in both highly acidic and highly alkaline environments suggests that H+ and OH− ions have minimal impact on the luminescence of CDs-AMP. As shown in Fig. 7d, the fluorescence intensity decreased by only 10 % in a 5 mol/L NaCl solution. The variation further underscores the potential of CDs-AMP for practical applications, demonstrating their robustness under diverse environmental conditions.

For the excellent optical performance of CDs-AMP under harsh conditions, we explored their potential application as probes for metal ions in water. A crucial aspect in determining the suitability of fluorescent probes is their selectivity. The selectivity of the synthesized CDs-AMP toward metal ions has been assessed (Fig. 8 a). The selectivity evaluation included cations such as Na+, Ca2+, K+, Ba2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Cd2+, Ag+, Co2+, and Mg2+, each at a concentration of 40 μM. The figure demonstrates that only Fe3+ exhibits quenching effect on the fluorescence of CDs-AMP among the various ions. Besides, after introducing 40 μM Fe3+ to each mixed-ion solution, a pronounced quenching effect was observed. Thus, in practical applications with these typical interfering ions, the synthesized CDs-AMP display excellent selectivity for Fe3+ detection.

The fluorescence emission spectra of CDs-AMP solutions in the presence of varying concentrations of Fe3+ ions were recorded. Under 330 nm excitation, the emission peak at 415 nm gradually decreased with increasing concentration of Fe3+ (Fig. 8b). A strong linear regression (R2 = 0.998) is observed between the quenching ratio [(F0 − F)/F0] and varying Fe3+ concentrations between 0 and 40 μM. The linear equation is (F0 − F)/F0 = 0.015 [Fe3+] − 0.002, where F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensities of CDs-AMP at 415 nm in the absence and presence of Fe3+, respectively. Under optimal experimental conditions, the detection limit (LOD) for Fe3+ using this method was determined to be 0.2 μM, based on the formula LOD = 3 S/N. With the concentration of iron ions increases, the absorption peaks at 305 nm, 320 nm, and 420 nm progressively increase (Fig. S5). These peaks were assigned to the n–π ∗ transitions of the carbonyl group and the surface groups present over the N-CDs. It is likely Fe3+ ions coordinate with the functional groups (amino and carboxyl) on the surface of CDs-AMP, forming charge transfer process between the CDs-AMP and Fe3+ ions, which consequently leads to fluorescence quench [40,41].

Real samples, including mineral, tap, and lake water, were analyzed to assess the precision and accuracy of this method. The standard addition technique was employed, and the analysis results are summarized in Table S4. Low RSD values indicated outstanding precision for Fe3+, with recovery rates ranging from 93.0 % to 103.0 %, indicating acceptable accuracy for the field analysis. The aforementioned results demonstrate that CDs-AMP hold great potential and exhibit high sensitivity in detecting Fe3+ concentrations in real-world samples.

In summary, dual-emissive CDs with varying spatial resistances were synthesized by adjusting the methyl content in the precursor materials. It was found that the fluorescence ratio of green-to-blue emission decreases as the methyl groups increase. We hypothesize that a FRET process occurs between the core states and surface states of the CDs. The presence of multiple methyl groups increases the spatial site resistance of the CDs, leads to high steric hindrance. The increase in steric hindrance results in a reduction of FRET efficiency from the blue band to the green band, causing a decrease in green fluorescence and a relative dominance of blue fluorescence peaks. The presence and impact of the FRET process was confirmed through analyses of spectral overlap and CDs spacing, and the FRET efficiency was calculated to support our hypothesis. What’s more, CDs-AMP, characterized by a high steric hindrance effect, exhibit remarkable stability under various challenging conditions, including prolonged storage, UV exposure, acidic and alkaline environments, and high-concentration salt solutions. These results provide a novel design of CDs with tunable photoluminescence properties by modulating the steric effects of surface functional groups, offering potential strategies of structural modifications for specific applications. Furthermore, a probe for Fe3+ ions in water based on CDs-AMP with high selectivity and sensitivity was constructed. The sensor has been successfully applied to the detection of Fe3+ ions in real samples including mineral, tap, and lake water, further proving its great potential in environmental monitoring.

Jun Xiao: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation. Yuan Jiang: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Yutong Lu: Validation, Formal analysis. Hui Zheng: Project administration. Peng Zheng: Visualization. Liang Zheng: Resources. Yang Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by Wenzhou Science & Technology Bureau Project s2023019.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2025.113910.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Jun Xiao is a master degree candidate of Faculty of Electronics Information, Hangzhou Dianzi University. He focuses on the fluorescence sensor of environment stimuli based on carbon materials.

Yuan Jiang is a lecturer in College of Electronics Information, Hangzhou Dianzi University. She received her Ph.D. degree from Southeast University in 2014. Her research interest includes functional nanomaterials and fluorescence sensors.
Yutong Lu is a master degree candidate of Faculty of Electronics Information, Hangzhou Dianzi University. He focuses on the research of nanomaterials synthesis and fluorescence applications.

Hui Zheng is an associate professor in Department of Electronics Information, Hangzhou Dianzi University. He received her Ph.D. degree from University of Electronic Science and Technology of China in 2016. His research interest includes carbon materials and dielectric materials.

Peng Zheng is an associate professor in College of Electronics Information, Hangzhou Dianzi University. He received her Ph.D. degree from Shandong University in 2010. His research interest includes optoelectronic devices based on carbon materials and dielectric materials.

Liang Zheng is a professor in College of Electronics Information, Hangzhou Dianzi University. He received her Ph.D. degree from Hangzhou Dianzi University in 2022. His research interest includes electron emission and electrical energy technology, microwave ferrite thin film device and Anti-EMI Technology.

Yang Zhang obtained his Ph.D. in Applied Physics from Beijing University of Technology in 1999. He conducted his postdoctoral research at the Department of Physics in The University of Georgia (USA) and served as a Visiting Scientist at the Department of Chemistry in Indiana University (Bloomington, USA) between 2005 and 2008. From 1999 to 2005, he held the position of vice director at the Center for Tsinghua University Photonics and Electronics. Currently, he is a professor at the School of Electronics Information in Hangzhou Dianzi University. His current research interests include carbon-based nanomaterials, field electron emission, 2D nanoelectronics: physics and devices of 2D materials, quantum nanostructures and sensors, and nano optoelectronics and photonics.
